item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the idea of any life in the SCP foundation to be able to live for ever which 1007 can if someone helps him but it requires work from the person helping him. Eventually that person's going to die, and soon all life will die, and no one will be able to bring him back. It works well in my opinion perfectly. Also any SCP being connected to Dr. Wondertainment is instantly wonderful SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Constant death after 75 minutes with the added bonus of going through immense pain in the first 16 minutes. Grand things being made by Dr.Wondertainment.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've read the SCP - nice, by the way - and a thought occured to me somewhat immediately: could we do anything to prolong this man's experience/existence? In a good way, I mean. There's the key… can it be turned by artificial means? If so, could a mechanical device be fashioned in such a way to rotate the key to counteract the rapid aging process? Unless of course, the decrease in age occurs in discrete decrements (ie. one year less for each complete turn exactly), in which case - never mind…

The Foundation may be cold and, for the most part, uncaring - but perhaps there could be further anomalous effects (or possible study avenues, maybe research on the human brain development?) which could be found, if the aging process could be significantly slowed?

Also, this might enable the Foundation to perform interesting live dissections, should the need arise - seeing as the effects can be readily reversed as the need arises. Possible SCP testing, including looking for cures? Using this person as a host to deadly diseases could help with developments that are not related to healing SCPs, but could instead be safely attributed to the normal advance of science.

Actually, can the aging process be slowed below normal if such a device could be constructed? Might we benefit from something like that? It is my understanding that the natural cognitive ability (information processing rate) of the subject is largely unaffected by the transformation (which is in itself quite anomalous!), on grounds of him being able to report pain in a way that would be meaningful to us…

Introducing further limitations (like, for example, requiring the man to sit in a chair that turns the key, permanently, due to the changes precluding any installations on the man's back) might be a good thought experiment…? Something along the lines of the cure being worse than the disease, in some aspect?

Someone would think of this if I did. I propose a solution be found that works into our current view on human life's value, or alternatively a good set of reasons as to why such would be unfeasible or plain impractical?

At the very least, such an experiment could be proposed and conducted, with possibly redacted (anti-tampering?) results…

Also, do we define ethics based on the sum total of 'experienced experiences', or based on memory? I mean, could we say that something was ethical if its effects on the person in question could be readily sent to oblivion by, literally, the turn of a magical key?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty depressing concept. I like it. Got my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but I'd like to see it expanded upon. Nonetheless, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the only interesting Safe SCPs.

…dude. Dude. Have you read many top-rated Safe SCPs?

Not that it's wrong to think Safe SCPs are boring, but c'mon. We've got some great reading here. Maybe read some more before giving up the possibility? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the only interesting Safe SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apparently, you aren't supposed to censor containment procedures. This is a relatively recent development, and the little misters were written before it was established. Originally, they were censored here, too, but I changed it to bring it in line with current standards. Clearly, the other little misters haven't been similarly updated, but as I did not write them I don't feel that it's my place to edit them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was looking through the Mr. collection and all the others have a black boxed hall and site number. Seems like an under the radar error? Or is this the reveal?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the old photo, and am disappointed to see it go, as well as the bit about clothing. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the SCP, but I'm not a huge fan of how the photograph has to be explained to fit in the article. It would work better for me if it was just a photo of an old man in pain or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Subject was on painkillers when photograph was taken"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I think this works best with the simplicity it has right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like more could be done with this. Does 1007 have a continuous memory, or is each cycle a different personality and set of memories? Can you turn the key the other way and make him age faster? If you make him age that way right before he dies, can you age him past 75, or would you just kill him several seconds faster? Could you make him decay faster using the key after he's died?

There's probably even more that hasn't occurred to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoy this, but the lack of facial censoring found in the other articles kinda bugs me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So he just… lives out an entire life being trapped in a coffin for 75 minutes. I…

I think I'm going to go hug my girlfriend.

Upvoted. Shiver.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent article. Do we know how SCP-1007 feels about his situation? I wonder what an interview log about his "death" would be like…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short and sweet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. I like the Wondertainment/MC&D interaction; it seems logical enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Roughly half the things I've seen MC&D get into seem to be a case of the classical Cabinet of Curiosities. Yeah, a good portion do have a good (or, at least, interesting) effect, but there's plenty of them which basically let the owner go "Hey, check out this strange thing, of which only one exists in the entire known universe!"

Then the Foundation comes along, grabs it, and throws it in a locked box in a warehouse buried half a mile underground.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's great is the fridge horror lurking in this article.

That being: What was the Marshall, Carter, and Dark customer *using* him for?

A person that can be any variable age, even if only for a short period of time…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahaha, this is brilliant Flibble. Good work. Easily upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to Salman Corbette for letting me use the Little Misters.
